column2: — Lilya!
You haven't changed at all!
Miyechka and Lilichka froze.
Marta stared straight ahead.
It seemed she was nauseous again.
— What, you didn't recognize me?
— the girl smiled broadly.
Against her tanned skin her teeth seemed snow-white.
— How are you, girls?
Lilichka and Miyechka exchanged glances, until the name 'Nastya' surfaced in Lilichka's memory.
Nastya had been the girlfriend of one of their old friends, who had gotten lost somewhere with the latest round of moves, the way things often get lost without a trace in moves and boxes.
Nastya smiled broadly and warmly, and Lilichka pulled out a chair for her at the table.
— You're exactly the same!
— she laughed, throwing her hand behind her neck.
— How are you?
— asked Miyechka.
— Just back from the Himalayas!
— Nastya laughed.
— We went there with Serhiy.
— Serhiy?
— asked Lilichka.
One of their mutual friends, lost without a trace, had also been called Serhiy.
— Oh, not that Serhiy, — said Nastya.
— It's just that this one is also called Serhiy.
We've been together for eleven years now.
Eleven!
— Congratulations, — said Miyechka.
— And how is it in the Himalayas?
— Oh, it's like this — you pay eight hundred dollars, and they lead you to enlightenment — everything just as you want.
If you want to find your destined partner — nine hundred, because scattered souls are harder to drag together, you understand?
— Aha, — Marta drawled.
— A hundred dollars harder.
— Yes, yes, — Nastya nodded energetically.
— Eight hundred is if the two of you go and want to hold out together longer.
— Well, you've held out a long time as it is, — said Lilichka.
— Well, eleven is me speaking figuratively.
We met back when that other Serhiy was still around, and dated for a few months, and then for nine years we weren't together physically, but mentally we were inseparable, you understand, girls?
Miyechka raised her eyebrows.
— And is there something there where they put some kind of vow on you, so you never meet anyone again?
— she asked.
— Oh, you'd have to ask about that, — Nastya reacted seriously.
You could hear that Ukrainian wasn't quite her native tongue, and she pronounced words and endings somewhat excessively.
— That costs three grand, — Marta spoke up.
— I don't know, that's maybe if it includes the flight, — said Nastya.
— And these drawings, you see, these, — Nastya put out into the middle of the table her tanned hands, covered with henna drawings.
— These things they do at the end for free!
Beautiful, isn't it?
— Mm-hm, — Lilichka nodded.
— It's not even just beautiful, — Nastya chirped.
— There are all kinds of meanings here too — this sun means that Serhiy and I will still be together for a whole year.
— A one-year guarantee?
— asked Miyechka.

— And next year you have to go again, because enlightenment, especially when it comes to scattered souls like Serhiy's and mine — well, not that Serhiy, but this one, the new one — is very complicated.
Because they say a person is born alone and dies alone, and only very powerful techniques can bind them to someone forever.
— And can you un-bind someone from someone there?
— asked Lilichka and glanced at her sister with a smile.
— I'll give you the phone number!
— Nastya exclaimed and began rummaging with her long, thin fingers in a red cloth bag with some exotic elephants on it.
— And how are you?
— she asked, not yet raising her head.
— How's Ivan?
— Nastya went on, casting a glance at Lilichka.
— Ivan...
— Lilichka began.
— Ivan's fine.
She tried to remember in what period and when Nastya had flashed past them, and who she was with back then.
— Don't you want to try the Himalayas?
— she said, holding out to Lilichka a business card on which a phone number was embossed in gold figures.
— Aiven went on a round-the-world trip by bicycle, or by moped, or on some other such two-wheeled thing, — said Miyechka.
— No, no, — Lilichka cut in.
— Aiven is the second Ivan, and Nastya is asking about the first Ivan, we — remember — went on a hike together back then, when Serhiy was still around.
— Which Serhiy?
— asked Nastya.
— Well, the one who disappeared.
— Disappeared where?
— Nastya goggled her eyes.
In that state they resembled two olives of the most expensive, largest varieties.
— Well, at least we don't know where he is.
— But he lives in that yard behind the kiosk, — Nastya waved her hand somewhere.
— In short, — Marta began.
— Aiven is her second husband, and the Ivan you're asking about had a child with some woman and left her with us, and I need to go to the toilet.

Marta, maintaining her dignity, got up from the table, revealing her already considerable belly, put on her dark glasses, licked her red, somewhat swollen lips, as happens with pregnant women, tucked behind her ear a strand of her heavy dark hair, which lay in a relentless black line just below her neck, and walked off.
Nastya fell silent.
Miyechka looked at the two women who remained at the table and said:
— She's a very good little child, this one of Ivan's.
— Mm-hm, — Lilichka nodded, grabbing the mug of long-since-finished coffee, — a girl.
— And how are you, Solomiya?
— Nastya tried to steer the conversation.
— Wonderful, — she said.
— How are things on the personal front?
'What a question…' Miyechka thought.
— Why are you here?
— Nastya pressed on relentlessly.
— I'm growing apples, — Solomiya answered and waved to the waiter with a smile.
— Bring me some white wine.
Anyone else?
— she glanced around the table.
— Go on, — retorted Lilichka.
— The sun's already on the decline, — she added, as if justifying herself to the enlightened Nastya.
— I won't, girls: my coach said that alcohol switches off your control, and if you want to build a connection with someone, you have to control that strictly.
Marta appeared in the terrace doorway with a bottle of sparkling mineral water.
— Oh!
— she said, looking at her sisters with the wine.
She managed to keep her straight back and her haughty gaze even now.
Pregnancy did not add any softness to her.
— What's new here?
Have you sorted out the Ivans and Serhiys yet?
— it seemed Marta felt relieved and her mood had improved.
— Miyechka was just telling us she's growing apples, — said Nastya.
— Are they organic?
— Our pumpkins are organic, — retorted Marta.
Meanwhile a tall man came up to the table in black sports shorts and a blue tank top, with sloping shoulders, a pronounced belly and thin legs.
He was one of those who always have dissatisfaction on their faces.
His shaved head revealed a low Neanderthal forehead.
— Seryozha!
— Nastya cried out.
The only thing that gave him away as Seryozha was the henna drawing on his hand.
Seryozha tried to keep his hand palm up.
His jaw was somewhat thrust forward, his brows barely fit between the line of his shaved hair and his eyes.
Seryozha stood in silence.
Marta looked the man over from head to toe, took a gulp of water from the bottle and went off to the toilet again.
Later in the car she confessed to Lilichka that Seryozha's bald head had stunk to her.
Seryozha bided his time.
He didn't sit down beside them, said nothing, didn't hear Nastya's chirping.
He just stood there, as if bewitched, until Nastya apologized.
— We have to go, girls, excuse us, — she said.
They took each other's hands, flowers were drawn in henna on both their hands, and left the terrace.
— Well, it helped, — said Lilichka.
— What helped?
— asked Miyechka.
— Well, it drew their souls together up there in the mountains.
A few thousand bucks — and there you go.
— And how could I un-bind my soul?
— Do you need to?
— I have problems, Lilya.
— I saw.
Your problem has a beautiful smile.
— He wrote that we won't see each other again.
— And nothing since then?
— We only talked about the apple trees.
— What does he say?
— He says it needs to be uprooted.
— I don't mean about the apples.
Just go and visit him.
— Listen, — Miyechka grabbed the cup with both hands.
— That only looks fitting in the movies.
In life no one waits for anyone.
In life I'll arrive, he won't be there, I'll sit by the entrance, then wander the city, get sweaty, with a wet back and legs dirty from the city dust.
And then he'll see me and won't hide the fact that this surprise isn't all that pleasant.
He's not one of those who hide things, Lilichka.
— You have problems, — replied Lilichka.
'Hortensia'
Miyechka wrote to Ustym from a café under his windows.
She got herself coffee she didn't want, but she needed something to hold in her hands and stir.
— Will you come down to me?
— she wrote.
— Where are you?
— I'm near you, — for certainty she put a full stop.
He came out about fifteen minutes later.
Miyechka guessed he'd been taking a shower.
Wet patches had come through on the back of his gray T-shirt.
— What do you want?
— he asked.
— I wanted to see you.
— How are the apple trees?
— Standing.
— You do understand, — he began, pulling out a chair.
— You understand that with these apple trees you're continuing the very same thing your grandfather started?
— What do you mean?
— You're doing what seems romantic to you for some reason, not what would be sensible.
— What kind of apple orchard would it be that comes up to my chest?
Ustym glanced at her chest.
Before setting out, Miyechka had spent a long time trying on everything she had, and settled on a heavenly-pink blouse the color of the blush on porcelain figurines of little girls.
— Do as you see fit.
Miyechka knew that he wouldn't talk about it anymore.
— You're not working today?
— she asked.
— I'm on vacation.
— Until when?
— Until whenever I want.
Ustym was occupied with something Miyechka understood nothing about.
He drove somewhere, flew somewhere, carried something, wrote something, sent something off, shouted at someone, and for some reason would suddenly start getting nervous in the middle of the night.
Sometimes she thought the worst.
— Let's go somewhere, — she said.
— Can't sit still at home?
Miyechka kept silent.
She looked under the table at her legs.
They were covered with city dust.
She felt a longing for the apple orchard.
— I promised you that we wouldn't see each other again.
